result:
I. A Door That Was Never There  
The first time I noticed the door, it was hanging in mid-air three inches above the carpet of my childhood bedroom. I was seven, it was past midnight, and the moon was a silver coin pressed against the window. The door had no frame, no wall; just a rectangle of oak with a porcelain knob the color of old teeth. It shouldn’t have been possible, but impossibility is a feeble objection to a seven-year-old who still believes his toys wake up when he leaves the room. I reached out, touched the knob, and felt it turn by itself. The door swung inward on hinges that sang like tuning forks. Behind it was not the hallway, not the upstairs bathroom, but a city street slick with rain, neon kanji smeared across puddles, and the smell of yakitori drifting from invisible stalls. I stepped through. The door closed. And for the next twenty-three years I forgot it had ever happened.

We forget the miraculous because remembering it would require us to redraw the map of our lives. Cartographers of the ordinary hate blank spaces labeled “here be dragons.” So the memory sank, a stone in the reservoir, until the day my father died and the executor handed me the keys to the house I had refused to enter since leaving for college. I walked into my old bedroom, now a storage unit of moldering board games and a crib I’d never assembled, and there was the door again—same height, same impossible levitation, same knob. It had been waiting patiently, the way certain stories wait for us to circle back with enough years in our pockets to pay the fare.

II. The City on the Other Side  
This time I was forty, divorced, acquainted with grief, so I took out my phone and recorded the door from every angle. The camera saw only drywall. I tried again; drywall. Technology, that obedient dog, refused to bark at the intruder. I opened the door.

The city had changed. Kanji had been replaced by Amharic syllables glowing blue on translucent awnings. The air smelled of coffee and frankincense. I walked barefoot on asphalt warm as skin. A woman with silver braids beckoned from a doorway curtained in bead strings. Inside, she poured tea that tasted of cedar and time. She spoke without moving her lips: “You’re late.” I tried to reply but produced no sound. She handed me a card the size of a playing card, blank except for a single drop of water that never dried. “Keep it wet,” she instructed, “and you’ll remember the way back.” Then she pushed me gently, and I was on the other side of the oak door again, standing in my dusty bedroom, card in hand, barefoot but no longer alone in the world.

III. The Cartography of Forgetting  
I became an apprentice to departure. Nights, I set an alarm for 2:00 a.m., opened the door, stepped onto streets that rearranged themselves like kaleidoscopes: Prague circa 1600, Lagos in 2096, Edo before the fire. I collected souvenirs: a tram ticket punched with holes that spelled a love letter; a seed that grew only in moonlight; a coin that bought nothing but the memory of your first betrayal. Each time I returned, the card was drier, the water drop shrinking like a waning moon. I tried sealing it in plastic, dripping tap water on it, even holding it in my mouth. Nothing arrested the evaporation. The woman had warned me: memory is a solvent; it dissolves what it preserves.

During the days I taught introductory philosophy to undergraduates who yawned at syllogisms but leaned forward when I mentioned the door. One student, Luz, stayed after class. She had eyes the color of wet ash and a habit of turning her pen into a tiny baton. “You’re not crazy,” she said. “My abuela has a mirror that shows tomorrow, but only between 3:07 and 3:08 a.m. We all inherit something that doesn’t believe in us.” She asked to see the door. I took her to the house. The door wasn’t there. Not invisible—absent. The wall behaved like a wall, stubbornly solid. Luz touched it, pressed her ear to the plaster. “Maybe it travels,” she whispered. “Maybe it’s ashamed.” I felt jilted, the way one feels when a private myth chooses someone else.

IV. The Archive of Almost  
Weeks bled. The card dried completely. The door never reappeared. I started dreaming in cities I hadn’t visited: narrow lanes paved with vinyl records, boulevards where streetlights were upside-down umbrellas glowing like jellyfish. I woke each morning with damp feet, as if I’d walked through puddles in my sleep. My neighbor, Mr. Wegener, an octogenarian who wore two watches set to different eras, caught me one dawn as I shook grit from my sheets. “You’ve been traveling,” he accused. I denied it. He lowered his voice. “I used to ride the elevator in the Mariana Trench. Third button down—past B2, before boiler room. It opened onto a ballroom filled with extinct birds. Dodo waltzes, passenger-pigeon polkas. Terrific band. One night I got off on the wrong floor: my ex-wife’s kitchen the day she left. Couldn’t stomach feathers after that.” He tapped my shoulder with a tremoring finger. “The bill comes due. Always.”

I began to compile an archive: photos of doorways that looked like they might be passages, fragments of maps from dreams, ticket stubs from buses that never existed. Luz helped. We met in the library’s sub-basement where the fluorescents hummed like trapped cicadas. She brought her grandmother’s mirror; I brought the dried card. At 3:07 we stared into the glass. For fifty-eight seconds it showed us only our reflections. At 3:08 it went dark. Luz turned it over; the backing had vanished, exposing gears made of paper. “Time to let go,” she said. But letting go is a skill taught by the same faculty that insists on clutching.

V. The Bureau of Unfinished Departures  
One December dusk, a postcard arrived addressed in my seven-year-old handwriting. Front: a photograph of the oak door. Back: “Wish you were here.” No stamp, no postmark. I returned to the house, stood in the bedroom, spoke to the empty wall. “I’m ready to pay the bill.” Nothing answered except the radiators clanking like old chains. I sat cross-legged, closed my eyes, and tried to remember the smell of yakitori, the cadence of Amharic neon, the weight of cedar tea. Memory felt like blowing dust off a mirror that kept cracking. Then I heard the hinge-song. The door was there, but smaller, doll-sized, hovering at knee height. I had to crawl. On the other side: not a city, but a corridor lined with shelves stretching into fog. Each shelf held an object abandoned mid-journey: a child’s left shoe still laced; a letter sealed with wax bearing no insignia; a snow globe inside which the snow had settled into the shape of a human heart. A plaque read: Bureau of Unfinished Departures—Please Sign In. A woman identical to the cedar-tea vendor sat behind a mahogany table, except her braids were ink-black. “You’re late again,” she said aloud, voice like a struck bell. I signed a ledger that dried my signature instantly, ink flaking into glitter. She handed me a droplet sealed in glass. “Last chance. Keep it whole.”

VI. The Rules of Return  
I asked the rules. She recited them like lullabies:  
1. You may travel only through doors you have already left.  
2. Every souvenir demands a tithe of equal weight.  
3. The droplet must remain intact; when it shatters, you forget the last thing that anchors you.  
I asked why me. She shrugged: “Doors are bored. They open for anyone who notices hinge-song.” I stepped back into my bedroom, clutching the glass bead. It felt heavier than galaxies. I placed it on the windowsill. Morning sun hit it; a rainbow scarred the wall. I taught my classes, graded papers, ate leftovers. Nights I checked: droplet intact. The door did not reappear. I began to suspect survival was a form of travel, each sunrise a country whose visa required only breathing.

VII. Luz’s Departure  
Luz stopped attending class. Email bounced. I went to her apartment—empty, lease terminated. On the floor lay her grandmother’s mirror, cracked into seven shards. Reflected in each: a different hour of the same Tuesday. I gathered them, cut my palm. Blood mirrored on glass looked like a door. I wrapped the shards in the sweater she’d left behind, placed them in a box, labeled it “Return to Sender.” The box gained weight nightly, as if she were inside adding stones. One dawn it was gone, only a pen-shaped baton on the windowsill. I twirled it; the droplet trembled but held.

VIII. The Tithing  
Spring arrived like a ransom note. My father’s estate finalized; I inherited debts I hadn’t known existed. Bills arrived stamped with symbols matching those on tram tickets from Prague. I understood: souvenirs were calling in their tithe. I opened the closet; half my clothes had vanished, replaced by objects I’d pocketed across worlds: the moon-seed, the betrayal-coin, the vinyl-pavement record. Each felt warm, pulsing like small hearts. I carried them to the bedroom, spoke to the wall. “Take what you need.” The droplet on the windowsill cracked; a single tear of water rolled out, evaporating before it reached wood. I forgot my father’s voice. Not the memory of him—just the timbre, the way he said my name like a question. A fair exchange, the bureau might say, but grief is a poor accountant.

IX. The Cartographer’s Rebellion  
I began mapping what I could not revisit. On butcher paper I drew coastlines of cities seen once, labeled them with names made of breathing: City of Cedar Aftertaste, City of Neon Lullabies. I pinned the maps on every wall. They overlapped like scars. Students wandered in during office hours, stared. Some wept without knowing why. One brought me a peach, laid it on the desk, left. That night I bit into it; the pit was a miniature staircase spiraling down. I planted it in a pot, watered it with the last of the glass-bead tear. A sprout emerged: seven leaves, each the shape of a different continent. On the underside of the largest: Luz’s handwriting—“Grow directions.”

X. The Harvest of Directions  
The plant grew overnight into a sapling, then a tree, pressing against the ceiling. Roots cracked the terracotta, dove through floorboards, seeking basement, seeking China, seeking molten core. Where roots surfaced, they spelled out departure times: 2:17, 3:07, 11:11. I packed a satchel: maps, pen-baton, mirror shards wrapped in Luz’s sweater. I pressed my ear to the trunk; inside, hinge-song. The tree was a door. I stepped through.

I landed not in a city but an ocean of tall grass under indigo sky. Each blade held a star, as if the sky had shattered and fallen like confetti. In the distance: a single elevator, doors open, cable disappearing into constellations. I walked. Inside: buttons labeled with every year I’d lived and some I hadn’t. I pressed 1987—the year I was seven. Doors closed. Weightlessness. Doors opened onto my childhood bedroom, the original door hovering mid-air. On the bed: me, age seven, asleep. In his hand: the blank card, now brimming with water. I reached; the pen-baton vibrated, became a fishing rod. I cast, hooked the card, pulled gently. It slid through dreaming fingers without waking him. Water glowed, a galaxy in miniature. I backed into the elevator, pressed NOW.

XI. The Return of the Late  
The elevator doors opened onto my office at the university. Students queued outside for final exams, unaware the room had been elsewhere. On the desk: the droplet restored, sealed now in crystal grown around it like a geode. I carried it to the classroom, set it on the windowsill. Luz walked in, hair longer, eyes same ash-wet color. She smiled as if she’d never vanished. After class she said, “I took the mirror to the post office for repairs. They shipped it to yesterday, but yesterday refused delivery, so it came back tomorrow. I hitched a ride.” We walked to the house. The bedroom wall was empty, no tree, no door. Only a new sprout peeking through floorboards: a peach pit cracking open. Luz knelt. “We start again,” she said. “But this time we write the rules where forgetfulness can’t reach.”

XII. The Rules Written in Sky  
We wrote them on butcher paper, pinned to the ceiling so the stars could proofread:  
1. Departures are not debts; they are seeds.  
2. Forgetting is a form of watering.  
3. Every door is a mirror held sideways by time.  
We signed our names in coffee grounds, in wax from a candle that burned backward. The droplet on the windowsill remained intact, but now it reflected not the sky but the hinge-song: a sound like every leaving and returning folded into a lullaby you remember only when you lose the words.

XIII. The House Without Walls  
Years later the house was demolished. Developers built condominiums named “The Maples.” On the lobby wall: a mosaic of a peach tree whose leaves spell departure times. Residents complain the fruit tastes of cedar and time; they wake with damp feet and carry starlight home in their pockets. They never find the source. But on certain nights, if you press your ear to the elevator button for the floor that doesn’t exist, you’ll hear hinge-song, and for an instant you’ll forget the last thing that anchored you—your father’s voice, your own reflection—and you’ll step through into tall grass under indigo sky, holding a card brimming with water that never dries, a pen-baton that writes maps on fog, and a name that is not quite yours but close enough to answer when the stars call you home.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
